Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent. I'd try to set aside time on weekends to catch up on everything, but it rarely worked out that way. Most weeks I'd watch lecture videos in chunks whenever I had free time between my extracurriculars and other commitments. I'm a visual learner, so I'd focus on diagrams and charts, but I wouldn't say I had a solid system going.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. I'd have these bursts of activity when assignments were due, then basically nothing for days. Regular study times just didn't happen for me this semester. Between everything else going on and honestly just not feeling that motivated, I'd study when I absolutely had to rather than on any schedule.
Q3: Usually a deadline would prompt me to log in. I'd get a notification or suddenly remember something was due soon and that's when I'd finally open the LMS. Sometimes my peer support group would mention something in our chat and I'd check it out. It wasn't really about wanting to learn, more about making sure I wasn't missing something important.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never felt like a habit, more like a chore I had to do. There were maybe a few weeks where I was logging in more regularly because of group discussions with my peers, but that faded. I'd go through phases where I'd forget it existed for a while.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I learn better visually. I could pause, rewind, and really look at the diagrams and examples they showed. The discussion forums were okay too because my peer group would post there and we'd help each other out. Those felt like they actually contributed to my understanding.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They were just walls of text and I couldn't stay focused on them. I'd download them thinking I'd read them later but they'd just sit in my downloads folder. The optional quizzes I also skipped most of the time because they felt pointless without grades attached.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help my grade, I'd do it. Also, things that were visual or interactive kept my attention better. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly relevant to passing, I just couldn't bring myself to care about.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely different. Required ones I'd force myself through even when bored out of my mind. Optional ones? I'd look at them, think 'maybe later,' and then never go back. Without that pressure of it affecting my grade, my low motivation just won. I know that's not great but that's how it was.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download the lecture slides and videos and study from those on my tablet without being connected. I also used YouTube videos to understand concepts better because sometimes the course explanations were dry. My peer group had a WhatsApp chat where we'd share notes and explanations, which helped a lot but obviously doesn't show up anywhere.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was studying from downloaded materials or working through problems with friends in person. We'd meet up sometimes and go over stuff together, which was actually more helpful than staring at the LMS alone. I'd also review my handwritten notes before exams, none of which would register as activity online.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download the slides, and then review those slides multiple times offline. It was easier on my eyes and I could study anywhere without worrying about internet. The LMS would only show that one view even though I'd gone over the material several times.
Q12: Around midterms it got really hard to stay engaged. I had a lot going on with my extracurriculars and the stress was building up. The course material started feeling repetitive and boring, which made it even harder to push through. My resilience isn't great, so when things piled up I just kind of shut down for a bit.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or stressed, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. I'd log in, stare at the screen, and just not absorb anything. Competing responsibilities from my activities meant the course often got pushed to the back burner. Confusion about some topics also made me avoid those sections entirely.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but be scrolling my phone or just zoning out completely. I'd technically be 'watching' but nothing was sticking. Sometimes I'd click through readings just to mark them complete without actually reading. It was like going through the motions without actually being there mentally.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they were important. The boredom just made it so hard to start anything. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, then tomorrow would come and I'd say the same thing. It's frustrating because I knew it was hurting my grades but I couldn't seem to break the cycle.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. My LMS activity would spike right before something was due. I'd cram everything into the last day or two, which was stressful but somehow it was the only way I could make myself work. Without a deadline looming, I just couldn't find the motivation.
Q17: Feedback had some effect but not as much as it probably should have. If I got decent feedback I'd feel okay and maybe put in a bit more effort next time. Bad feedback honestly just made me feel worse and less motivated. The peer support helped more than instructor feedback because it felt less judgmental.
Q18: The course structure was okay but it didn't really push me to stay consistent. Having everything available at once meant I could always say 'I'll do it later.' If it had been more structured with weekly requirements, I might have kept up better. The flexibility actually worked against me given my motivation issues.
Q19: The video content being well-organized with good visuals made engagement easier for me. But the heavy reading requirements made it harder since that's not how I learn best. Also, some modules felt disconnected from each other, which made it hard to see the point. More interactive elements would have helped keep me interested.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's clicking around, maybe watching videos but not really absorbing anything, or just checking what's due without actually completing work. You can spend a lot of time on a platform without being productive. Boredom and low motivation can lead to a lot of aimless browsing.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. I did a lot of studying offline with downloaded materials and with my peer group. My exam scores were decent enough to pass even though my attendance and LMS activity were low. The platform doesn't capture studying that happens outside of it, which was significant for me.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, which gives a basic picture of activity patterns. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you were actually focused or just going through the motions. They also miss all the offline work and peer collaboration that happens outside the system.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials. For me, real engagement happened more in conversations with my peer group than alone on the LMS. It's about being mentally present, not just physically or digitally present.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support group helped me get through the course. It wouldn't show the offline studying or the times I was genuinely trying to understand something even if I wasn't logged in. It also can't capture how bored and unmotivated I felt most of the time, which affected everything but isn't visible in click data.
